title: Event-Driven Web Development
description: Build reactive, decoupled systems with events -- pub/sub patterns, message queues, webhooks, WebSockets, Server-Sent Events, and event sourcing
keyPoints: Event-driven architecture decouples producers from consumers, letting systems scale and evolve independently
Pub/sub and message queues buffer work and guarantee delivery even when consumers crash or lag behind
WebSockets provide full-duplex real-time communication while Server-Sent Events offer simpler one-way push
Webhooks let external services notify your app of events via HTTP callbacks with signature verification
Event sourcing stores every state change as an immutable event, enabling audit trails and time-travel debugging

blocks.heading: What is Event-Driven Architecture?
blocks.body: In traditional request/response architecture, component A calls component B directly. A knows about B, waits for B to respond, and fails if B is down. In event-driven architecture, A emits an event -- 'something happened' -- and any interested component listens and reacts independently. A does not know or care who is listening. This creates loose coupling, better scalability, and systems that are easier to extend.

Think of it like a radio broadcast. The radio station (producer) transmits a signal. It does not know how many radios are tuned in, or what people do when they hear the news. Listeners (consumers) independently decide to react -- maybe one person checks the weather forecast, another changes the channel. The station and listeners are completely decoupled.

cards.title: Request / Response
cards.body: Synchronous and tightly coupled. Component A calls B directly, waits for a response, and fails if B is unavailable. Simple to reason about but creates hard dependencies.

cards.title: Event-Driven
cards.body: Asynchronous and loosely coupled. A emits an event, any number of listeners react independently. A does not wait and does not know who is listening. Easy to extend.

cards.title: Message Queue
cards.body: Buffered and guaranteed delivery. Messages sit in a queue until a consumer processes them. If the consumer crashes, the message stays queued. Enables reliable background work.

cards.title: Stream Processing
cards.body: Ordered and replayable. Events are appended to a log. Consumers can read from any point. Ideal for analytics pipelines, IoT data, and audit trails. Think Kafka.

blocks.heading: When to Use Event-Driven vs Request/Response
rows: Fetching a user profile for display
You need the data right now to render the page. Synchronous is simpler.
Sending a welcome email after signup
The signup response should not wait for the email to send. Fire and forget.
CPU-intensive work should not block the upload response. Queue it for a worker.
Both sides need to send messages at any time. Persistent bidirectional connection.
Server pushes updates. Client only needs to listen. SSE is simpler than WebSocket.
Stripe notifying you of a payment
External service POSTs to your endpoint when an event occurs. You react.
You need a complete history of every change, not just the current state.

blocks.heading: Frontend Events -- The Browser Event System
blocks.body: The browser is already event-driven. Every click, keypress, scroll, and network response is an event. The DOM event system -- addEventListener, event bubbling, capturing -- is the foundation that all frontend event patterns build on. Understanding it deeply is essential before building custom event systems.

blocks.description: DOM event handling -- addEventListener, event delegation, custom events, and EventTarget for custom classes
Custom typed EventEmitter class with subscribe, unsubscribe, and emit using TypeScript generics
Using events in React -- synthetic events, handlers, bubbling, and useEffect cleanup for global events

blocks.title: Event Delegation Pattern -- Why One Listener Beats a Thousand

blocks.body: Imagine a todo list with 1,000 items, each with a delete button. You could attach 1,000 click handlers -- one per button. Every time you add or remove a todo, you must attach or detach a handler. This is wasteful and error-prone.

With event delegation, you attach **one** click handler to the parent `<ul>`. When any child button is clicked, the event bubbles up to the parent. You inspect `event.target` to determine which button was clicked. This uses less memory, handles dynamically added items automatically, and is simpler to manage.

blocks.description: Event delegation with one parent listener

blocks.heading: In-App Event Bus (Frontend)
blocks.body: An event bus decouples React components that do not share a parent. Instead of drilling props through five layers or wiring up context for one-off communication, Component A emits 'cart:updated' and Component B (the header cart badge) reacts -- without either knowing the other exists.

blocks.description: Typed EventBus singleton -- shared instance for cross-component communication
useEventBus hook -- subscribe in useEffect with auto-cleanup, emit from any component
Real example -- Shopping cart event flow: AddToCartButton emits, CartBadge, CartDrawer, and Analytics all react independently

blocks.title: Do Not Overuse the Event Bus
blocks.body: Event buses can become 'invisible spaghetti' if overused. When any component can emit anything and any component can listen to anything, the flow of data becomes very hard to trace and debug. Use the event bus for truly cross-cutting concerns (analytics, notifications, auth state). For most component communication, prefer props, React context, or a state manager like Redux or Zustand.

blocks.heading: Backend Event System (Python)
blocks.body: Server-side events decouple business logic in the same way. When a user registers, you do not want the registration endpoint to also send emails, create profiles, award XP, and log analytics all inline. That creates a monolithic function that grows without bound. Instead, emit a 'user.registered' event and let independent handlers react.

blocks.description: Simple synchronous event dispatcher class with on() and emit()
Flask integration -- event-driven user registration with independent listeners
blocks.code:
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
from event_dispatcher import dispatcher
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
app = Flask(__name__)


# --- Register event handlers independently ---

def send_welcome_email(user_id: str, email: str, **kwargs):
    """Send welcome email. Does not block registration if it fails."""
    logger.info(f"Sending welcome email to {email}")
    # In production: use an email service like SendGrid
    # requests.post("https://api.sendgrid.com/v3/mail/send", ...)


def create_user_profile(user_id: str, email: str, **kwargs):
    """Create a default profile for the new user."""
    logger.info(f"Creating profile for user {user_id}")
    # db.session.add(UserProfile(user_id=user_id, display_name=email.split('@')[0]))
    # db.session.commit()


def award_signup_xp(user_id: str, **kwargs):
    """Award XP points for signing up."""
    logger.info(f"Awarding 100 XP to user {user_id}")
    # xp_service.award(user_id, points=100, reason="signup")


def log_registration_analytics(user_id: str, email: str, **kwargs):
    """Track registration in analytics."""
    logger.info(f"Analytics: user.registered user_id={user_id}")
    # analytics.track("user.registered", {"user_id": user_id})


# Wire up handlers -- each is independent
dispatcher.on("user.registered", send_welcome_email)
dispatcher.on("user.registered", create_user_profile)
dispatcher.on("user.registered", award_signup_xp)
dispatcher.on("user.registered", log_registration_analytics)


@app.post("/api/register")
def register():
    data = request.get_json()
    email = data["email"]
    password = data["password"]

    # Core registration logic
    user_id = create_user_in_db(email, password)  # Your DB logic

    # Emit event -- all handlers react independently
    dispatcher.emit("user.registered", user_id=user_id, email=email)

    return jsonify({"user_id": user_id}), 201

blocks.description: Decorator pattern for registering event handlers cleanly
Synchronous event dispatch -- handlers run inline in the same request
blocks.code:
from event_dispatcher import dispatcher

# Handlers run in the same thread, same request.
# The HTTP response waits until ALL handlers finish.

@app.post("/api/register")
def register():
    user_id = create_user(request.json)

    # These all run synchronously, one after another.
    # If send_email takes 3 seconds, the response is delayed 3 seconds.
    dispatcher.emit("user.registered", user_id=user_id, email=request.json["email"])

    return jsonify({"user_id": user_id}), 201

# Pros: Simple, easy to debug, errors are visible immediately.
# Cons: Slow handlers block the response. One failing handler
#        can break the request if not caught.

blocks.description: Fire-and-forget with threads -- non-blocking but limited reliability
Celery task queue -- reliable background processing with retries

blocks.heading: WebSockets -- Bidirectional Real-Time
blocks.body: WebSockets maintain a persistent connection between browser and server. Unlike HTTP, where the client must ask for data each time, WebSockets let both sides send messages at any time. This makes them ideal for chat apps, live updates, collaborative editing, multiplayer games, and any scenario where both client and server need to push data.

blocks.description: Flask-SocketIO server with namespaces, rooms, broadcast, and authentication
React WebSocket hook with auto-reconnect, heartbeat, connection status, and typed messages
Full working chat room -- React client with rooms, messages, typing indicator, and online users

blocks.heading: WebSocket Events Lifecycle
rows: Update UI to 'connected', join rooms, fetch initial data
Display in UI (client) or broadcast to room (server)
Connection attempt fails (bad auth, server down)
Show error message, retry with backoff

blocks.heading: Server-Sent Events (SSE) -- One-Way Push
blocks.body: Server-Sent Events are simpler than WebSockets when you only need server-to-client push. SSE is built on top of HTTP, automatically reconnects if the connection drops, works through proxies and load balancers, and uses a simple text-based protocol. It is ideal for live dashboards, notification feeds, progress bars, and any scenario where the server pushes updates but the client does not need to send data back over the same connection.

blocks.description: Flask SSE endpoint with streaming response, event types, and retry intervals
EventSource hook with auto-reconnect, event type filtering, and error handling

rows: Bidirectional -- both client and server can send at any time
ws:// or wss:// -- upgrades from HTTP to a persistent TCP connection
Must be implemented manually or with a library (socket.io handles it)
Can be tricky -- some proxies and firewalls block WebSocket upgrades
Chat, games, collaborative editing -- both sides send frequently
Higher -- requires WebSocket server, connection management, heartbeats
One-way -- server pushes to client only. Client uses regular HTTP for requests.
Standard HTTP with text/event-stream content type
Built-in -- the browser automatically reconnects with Last-Event-ID header
Excellent -- works through any HTTP proxy, CDN, or load balancer
Live dashboards, notifications, progress updates -- server pushes, client listens
Low -- just a streaming HTTP response on the server, EventSource API on the client
Simulated push -- client makes repeated requests, server holds the response until data is available
Standard HTTP -- no special protocol or API needed
Built into the pattern -- client immediately sends a new request after each response
Universal -- plain HTTP requests work everywhere
Fallback when SSE or WebSocket is not available. Legacy browser support.
Medium -- simple concept but wastes server resources holding connections open

blocks.heading: Message Queues -- Guaranteed Delivery
blocks.body: Message queues (Redis, RabbitMQ, SQS) sit between producers and consumers to guarantee delivery. If a consumer crashes, the message stays in the queue and is redelivered. If the producer sends messages faster than the consumer can handle, the queue buffers them. This is essential for any background work that must not be lost -- email sending, image processing, payment handling.

blocks.description: Redis pub/sub -- publisher and subscriber with redis-py
blocks.code:
import redis
import json
import threading

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)


# --- Publisher ---
def publish_event(channel: str, data: dict) -> int:
    """Publish an event to a Redis channel. Returns number of subscribers who received it."""
    message = json.dumps(data)
    num_receivers = r.publish(channel, message)
    print(f"Published to '{channel}': {data} ({num_receivers} receivers)")
    return num_receivers


# --- Subscriber ---
def subscribe_to_events(channels: list[str], handler):
    """Subscribe to Redis channels and call handler for each message."""
    pubsub = r.pubsub()
    pubsub.subscribe(*channels)

    print(f"Listening on channels: {channels}")
    for message in pubsub.listen():
        if message["type"] == "message":
            channel = message["channel"]
            data = json.loads(message["data"])
            handler(channel, data)


# --- Usage ---
def handle_event(channel: str, data: dict):
    print(f"Received on '{channel}': {data}")


# Run subscriber in a background thread
subscriber_thread = threading.Thread(
    target=subscribe_to_events,
    args=(["orders", "payments"], handle_event),
    daemon=True,
)
subscriber_thread.start()

# Publish events
publish_event("orders", {"order_id": "ORD-001", "action": "created", "total": 99.99})
publish_event("payments", {"order_id": "ORD-001", "status": "completed"})

# NOTE: Redis pub/sub does NOT persist messages. If no subscriber is
# listening when a message is published, it is lost. For guaranteed
# delivery, use Redis Streams, Celery, or a proper message broker.

blocks.description: Celery task queue with Redis -- define tasks, send async, handle results, retry on failure
blocks.code:
from celery import Celery, chain, group

celery_app = Celery(
    "worker",
    broker="redis://localhost:6379/0",
    backend="redis://localhost:6379/1",  # Store results
)

celery_app.conf.update(
    task_serializer="json",
    result_serializer="json",
    accept_content=["json"],
    timezone="UTC",
    task_track_started=True,
    task_acks_late=True,       # Acknowledge only after task completes
    worker_prefetch_multiplier=1,  # Fair dispatch
)


@celery_app.task(bind=True, max_retries=3)
def resize_image(self, image_path: str, sizes: list[tuple[int, int]]):
    """Resize an image to multiple sizes."""
    try:
        from PIL import Image
        img = Image.open(image_path)
        results = []
        for width, height in sizes:
            resized = img.resize((width, height))
            output_path = f"{image_path}_{width}x{height}.jpg"
            resized.save(output_path)
            results.append(output_path)
        return results
    except Exception as exc:
        raise self.retry(exc=exc, countdown=2 ** self.request.retries * 10)


@celery_app.task
def generate_thumbnail(image_path: str) -> str:
    """Generate a thumbnail for an image."""
    from PIL import Image
    img = Image.open(image_path)
    img.thumbnail((200, 200))
    thumb_path = f"{image_path}_thumb.jpg"
    img.save(thumb_path)
    return thumb_path


@celery_app.task
def update_database(image_id: str, paths: list[str]) -> dict:
    """Update the database with processed image paths."""
    # db.execute("UPDATE images SET processed_paths = ? WHERE id = ?", ...)
    return {"image_id": image_id, "paths": paths, "status": "complete"}


# --- Sending tasks ---
# Simple async call:
result = resize_image.delay("/uploads/photo.jpg", [(800, 600), (400, 300)])
print(result.id)         # Task ID
print(result.status)     # PENDING -> STARTED -> SUCCESS
print(result.get(timeout=30))  # Wait for result (blocking)

# Chain tasks (pipeline):
pipeline = chain(
    resize_image.s("/uploads/photo.jpg", [(800, 600)]),
    generate_thumbnail.s(),  # Receives output of previous task
)
pipeline.apply_async()

# Run tasks in parallel:
batch = group(
    resize_image.s("/uploads/photo1.jpg", [(800, 600)]),
    resize_image.s("/uploads/photo2.jpg", [(800, 600)]),
    resize_image.s("/uploads/photo3.jpg", [(800, 600)]),
)
results = batch.apply_async()

blocks.description: Real example -- Image upload pipeline: API enqueues task, Celery worker processes, emits completion event
blocks.code:
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
from celery import chain
from celery_tasks import celery_app, resize_image, generate_thumbnail, update_database
from event_dispatcher import dispatcher
import uuid
import os

app = Flask(__name__)
UPLOAD_DIR = "/var/uploads"


@celery_app.task
def emit_image_processed(result: dict, image_id: str):
    """Final task in the chain -- emits an event when processing is complete."""
    dispatcher.emit("image.processed", image_id=image_id, paths=result["paths"])
    return result


@app.post("/api/images/upload")
def upload_image():
    """Accept an image upload and enqueue processing."""
    file = request.files["image"]
    image_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    file_path = os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, f"{image_id}.jpg")
    file.save(file_path)

    # Build a processing pipeline:
    # 1. Resize to standard sizes
    # 2. Generate thumbnail
    # 3. Update database
    # 4. Emit completion event
    pipeline = chain(
        resize_image.s(file_path, [(1200, 900), (800, 600), (400, 300)]),
        generate_thumbnail.s(),
        update_database.s(image_id),
        emit_image_processed.s(image_id),
    )

    # Enqueue -- returns immediately. Worker processes in background.
    task_result = pipeline.apply_async()

    return jsonify({
        "image_id": image_id,
        "task_id": task_result.id,
        "status": "processing",
        "status_url": f"/api/images/{image_id}/status",
    }), 202  # 202 Accepted -- processing is not yet complete


@app.get("/api/images/<image_id>/status")
def image_status(image_id: str):
    """Check the processing status of an uploaded image."""
    # In production, query the database for the image status
    return jsonify({"image_id": image_id, "status": "processing"})

cards.title: Redis Pub/Sub
cards.body: Simple, fast, no persistence. Messages are lost if no subscriber is listening. Best for real-time notifications where missing a message is acceptable.

cards.title: Celery + Redis
cards.body: Python task queue with retries, result tracking, scheduling, and chaining. The go-to for Flask and Django background jobs.

cards.title: RabbitMQ
cards.body: Enterprise message broker with routing, exchanges, dead letter queues, and AMQP protocol. More complex but more powerful than Redis.

cards.title: AWS SQS
cards.body: Fully managed cloud queue. No infrastructure to maintain. Pay per message. Great for serverless architectures and Lambda functions.

cards.title: Apache Kafka
cards.body: Distributed event streaming platform. Ordered, persistent, replayable logs. Built for high-throughput analytics, IoT, and event sourcing at scale.

blocks.heading: Webhooks -- Events Between Services
blocks.body: Webhooks are HTTP callbacks. When something happens in Service A (a payment succeeds on Stripe, a push is made on GitHub, a message is sent on Slack), it POSTs a JSON payload to a URL you registered. Your server receives the POST, verifies it is authentic, and processes the event. This is how most third-party APIs notify your application of events without requiring you to poll.

blocks.description: Flask webhook receiver with HMAC signature verification for security
blocks.code:
import hmac
import hashlib
import json
import time
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify, abort
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
app = Flask(__name__)

WEBHOOK_SECRET = "whsec_your_webhook_signing_secret"


def verify_signature(payload: bytes, signature: str, secret: str) -> bool:
    """Verify HMAC-SHA256 signature of webhook payload."""
    expected = hmac.new(
        secret.encode("utf-8"),
        payload,
        hashlib.sha256,
    ).hexdigest()
    # Use constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks
    return hmac.compare_digest(f"sha256={expected}", signature)


def verify_timestamp(timestamp: str, tolerance_seconds: int = 300) -> bool:
    """Reject webhooks older than tolerance (prevent replay attacks)."""
    webhook_time = int(timestamp)
    current_time = int(time.time())
    return abs(current_time - webhook_time) <= tolerance_seconds


@app.post("/webhooks/provider")
def receive_webhook():
    """Generic webhook receiver with signature and timestamp verification."""
    payload = request.get_data()
    signature = request.headers.get("X-Webhook-Signature", "")
    timestamp = request.headers.get("X-Webhook-Timestamp", "0")

    # Step 1: Verify timestamp (prevent replay attacks)
    if not verify_timestamp(timestamp):
        logger.warning("Webhook rejected: timestamp too old")
        abort(401, "Timestamp too old")

    # Step 2: Verify signature (ensure it came from the real provider)
    if not verify_signature(payload, signature, WEBHOOK_SECRET):
        logger.warning("Webhook rejected: invalid signature")
        abort(401, "Invalid signature")

    # Step 3: Parse the event
    event = json.loads(payload)
    event_type = event.get("type")
    logger.info(f"Webhook received: {event_type}")

    # Step 4: Handle the event
    handlers = {
        "order.created": handle_order_created,
        "order.completed": handle_order_completed,
        "payment.failed": handle_payment_failed,
    }

    handler = handlers.get(event_type)
    if handler:
        handler(event["data"])
    else:
        logger.info(f"Unhandled webhook event type: {event_type}")

    # Step 5: Return 200 quickly. Process async if needed.
    return jsonify({"received": True}), 200


def handle_order_created(data: dict):
    logger.info(f"Order created: {data['order_id']}")

def handle_order_completed(data: dict):
    logger.info(f"Order completed: {data['order_id']}")

def handle_payment_failed(data: dict):
    logger.info(f"Payment failed for order: {data['order_id']}")

blocks.description: Webhook sender -- register URLs, queue deliveries, retry with exponential backoff
blocks.code:
import hmac
import hashlib
import json
import time
import requests
import logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


@dataclass
class WebhookSubscription:
    url: str
    secret: str
    events: list[str]  # Event types this subscription cares about
    active: bool = True
    failure_count: int = 0
    max_failures: int = 10  # Disable after 10 consecutive failures


@dataclass
class WebhookDelivery:
    subscription: WebhookSubscription
    event_type: str
    payload: dict
    attempt: int = 0
    max_attempts: int = 5
    created_at: float = field(default_factory=time.time)


class WebhookSender:
    def __init__(self):
        self.subscriptions: list[WebhookSubscription] = []

    def register(self, url: str, secret: str, events: list[str]) -> WebhookSubscription:
        sub = WebhookSubscription(url=url, secret=secret, events=events)
        self.subscriptions.append(sub)
        return sub

    def send(self, event_type: str, data: dict):
        """Send an event to all subscribed webhooks."""
        for sub in self.subscriptions:
            if not sub.active or event_type not in sub.events:
                continue
            delivery = WebhookDelivery(
                subscription=sub, event_type=event_type, payload=data,
            )
            self._deliver(delivery)

    def _deliver(self, delivery: WebhookDelivery):
        sub = delivery.subscription
        payload = json.dumps({
            "type": delivery.event_type,
            "data": delivery.payload,
            "timestamp": int(time.time()),
        })

        # Sign the payload
        signature = "sha256=" + hmac.new(
            sub.secret.encode(), payload.encode(), hashlib.sha256,
        ).hexdigest()

        try:
            response = requests.post(
                sub.url,
                data=payload,
                headers={
                    "Content-Type": "application/json",
                    "X-Webhook-Signature": signature,
                    "X-Webhook-Timestamp": str(int(time.time())),
                },
                timeout=10,
            )
            response.raise_for_status()
            sub.failure_count = 0  # Reset on success
            logger.info(f"Webhook delivered to {sub.url}")

        except requests.RequestException as e:
            delivery.attempt += 1
            sub.failure_count += 1
            logger.warning(f"Webhook delivery failed ({delivery.attempt}/{delivery.max_attempts}): {e}")

            if sub.failure_count >= sub.max_failures:
                sub.active = False
                logger.error(f"Webhook disabled after {sub.max_failures} failures: {sub.url}")
            elif delivery.attempt < delivery.max_attempts:
                # Exponential backoff: 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 32s
                delay = 2 ** delivery.attempt
                logger.info(f"Retrying webhook in {delay}s")
                # In production, use Celery: deliver_webhook.apply_async(countdown=delay)


# --- Usage ---
sender = WebhookSender()
sender.register(
    url="https://myapp.com/webhooks/orders",
    secret="whsec_abc123",
    events=["order.created", "order.completed"],
)
sender.send("order.created", {"order_id": "ORD-42", "total": 99.99})

blocks.title: Register Webhook Endpoint URL
blocks.body: In your Stripe dashboard, go to Developers > Webhooks and add your endpoint URL (e.g., https://yourapp.com/webhooks/stripe). Select the event types you want to receive (payment_intent.succeeded, customer.subscription.created, etc.). Copy the webhook signing secret.
blocks.code:
# Your endpoint URL must be HTTPS in production.
# For local development, use a tunnel:
# ngrok http 5000
# Then use the ngrok URL: https://abc123.ngrok.io/webhooks/stripe

blocks.title: Create Flask Route to Receive POST
blocks.body: Create an endpoint that accepts POST requests from Stripe. The raw request body is needed for signature verification, so read it before parsing JSON.

blocks.title: Verify Stripe Signature
blocks.body: Stripe signs every webhook with your endpoint secret. Always verify this signature before trusting the payload. This prevents attackers from sending fake events to your endpoint.

blocks.title: Parse Event Type and Route to Handler
blocks.body: Stripe sends many event types. Parse the type and route to the appropriate handler function.
blocks.code:
event_type = event["type"]
data = event["data"]["object"]

handlers = {
    "payment_intent.succeeded": handle_payment_success,
    "payment_intent.payment_failed": handle_payment_failure,
    "customer.subscription.created": handle_new_subscription,
    "customer.subscription.deleted": handle_cancelled_subscription,
    "invoice.payment_failed": handle_invoice_failure,
}

blocks.title: Handle Each Event Type
blocks.body: Implement handler functions for each event type your app cares about. Each handler should be idempotent -- safe to call multiple times with the same event.
blocks.code:
def handle_payment_success(data: dict):
    payment_intent_id = data["id"]
    amount = data["amount"] / 100  # Stripe amounts are in cents
    customer_id = data["customer"]
    # Update order status, send receipt, unlock access, etc.
    logger.info(f"Payment succeeded: {payment_intent_id} (${amount})")

def handle_new_subscription(data: dict):
    subscription_id = data["id"]
    customer_id = data["customer"]
    plan_id = data["items"]["data"][0]["price"]["id"]
    # Activate the subscription in your database
    logger.info(f"New subscription: {subscription_id} plan={plan_id}")

blocks.title: Return 200 Quickly
blocks.body: Stripe expects a 200 response within 20 seconds. If your processing takes longer, return 200 immediately and process asynchronously (e.g., with Celery). Stripe will retry failed deliveries with exponential backoff.

blocks.description: Complete Stripe webhook handler with signature verification, event routing, and idempotency
blocks.code:
import stripe
import logging
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify, abort
from functools import wraps

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
app = Flask(__name__)

stripe.api_key = "sk_test_your_key_here"
ENDPOINT_SECRET = "whsec_your_webhook_secret"

# Track processed events for idempotency
processed_events: set[str] = set()  # In production, use a database table


@app.post("/webhooks/stripe")
def stripe_webhook():
    payload = request.get_data()
    sig_header = request.headers.get("Stripe-Signature")

    # Verify signature
    try:
        event = stripe.Webhook.construct_event(payload, sig_header, ENDPOINT_SECRET)
    except ValueError:
        logger.warning("Invalid webhook payload")
        abort(400)
    except stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError:
        logger.warning("Invalid webhook signature")
        abort(401)

    # Idempotency check -- skip if we already processed this event
    event_id = event["id"]
    if event_id in processed_events:
        logger.info(f"Skipping duplicate event: {event_id}")
        return jsonify({"received": True, "duplicate": True}), 200

    # Route to handler
    event_type = event["type"]
    data = event["data"]["object"]

    handlers = {
        "payment_intent.succeeded": handle_payment_success,
        "payment_intent.payment_failed": handle_payment_failure,
        "customer.subscription.created": handle_new_subscription,
        "customer.subscription.deleted": handle_cancelled_subscription,
        "invoice.payment_failed": handle_invoice_failure,
    }

    handler = handlers.get(event_type)
    if handler:
        try:
            handler(data)
            processed_events.add(event_id)  # Mark as processed
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error(f"Error handling {event_type}: {e}", exc_info=True)
            # Return 200 anyway -- we received it. Fix the bug and replay.
    else:
        logger.info(f"Unhandled event type: {event_type}")

    return jsonify({"received": True}), 200


def handle_payment_success(data: dict):
    payment_id = data["id"]
    amount = data["amount"] / 100
    customer = data.get("customer")
    logger.info(f"Payment succeeded: {payment_id} ${amount} customer={customer}")
    # Update order: db.orders.update({"payment_intent": payment_id}, {"status": "paid"})
    # Send receipt email: send_receipt.delay(payment_id)


def handle_payment_failure(data: dict):
    payment_id = data["id"]
    error = data.get("last_payment_error", {}).get("message", "Unknown error")
    logger.warning(f"Payment failed: {payment_id} - {error}")
    # Notify customer: send_payment_failed_email.delay(payment_id, error)


def handle_new_subscription(data: dict):
    sub_id = data["id"]
    customer = data["customer"]
    price_id = data["items"]["data"][0]["price"]["id"]
    logger.info(f"New subscription: {sub_id} customer={customer} price={price_id}")
    # Activate: db.subscriptions.insert({"stripe_id": sub_id, ...})


def handle_cancelled_subscription(data: dict):
    sub_id = data["id"]
    logger.info(f"Subscription cancelled: {sub_id}")
    # Deactivate: db.subscriptions.update({"stripe_id": sub_id}, {"active": False})


def handle_invoice_failure(data: dict):
    invoice_id = data["id"]
    customer = data["customer"]
    logger.warning(f"Invoice payment failed: {invoice_id} customer={customer}")
    # Alert: send_invoice_failed_notification.delay(customer, invoice_id)

blocks.title: Always Verify Webhook Signatures
blocks.body: Without signature verification, anyone can POST fake events to your webhook endpoint. An attacker could send a fake 'payment_intent.succeeded' event to grant themselves access to paid features. Always verify the HMAC-SHA256 signature using the provider's webhook secret. This is not optional -- it is a critical security requirement.

items: **Signature Verification**: Always verify HMAC-SHA256 signatures using the provider's webhook secret. Use constant-time comparison (hmac.compare_digest) to prevent timing attacks.
**Idempotency Keys**: Track processed event IDs in a database. Webhooks can be delivered more than once. Your handler must be safe to call multiple times with the same event.
**Replay Protection**: Check the webhook timestamp. Reject events older than 5 minutes to prevent replay attacks where an attacker resends a captured webhook.
**IP Allowlisting**: If the provider publishes a list of IP addresses their webhooks come from, configure your firewall or reverse proxy to only accept traffic from those IPs.
**HTTPS Only**: Never expose a webhook endpoint over plain HTTP. TLS encrypts the payload in transit and prevents man-in-the-middle interception.
**Return 200 Quickly**: Providers typically retry if they do not receive a 200 within a few seconds. If processing takes time, return 200 immediately and process asynchronously.
**Log Everything**: Log the event type, event ID, and processing result for every webhook received. You will need this for debugging and reconciliation.

blocks.heading: Event Sourcing
blocks.body: Traditional databases store current state. You update a row, and the previous value is gone. Event sourcing takes a different approach: instead of storing current state, you store every event that changed the state. The current state is derived by replaying events from the beginning. This gives you a complete audit trail, the ability to time-travel to any point in history, and the option to replay events to build new projections.

blocks.description: Simple event store -- append events, retrieve by stream, rebuild state by replaying
Shopping cart as an event stream -- replay events to rebuild current state
blocks.code:
from event_store import EventStore, rebuild_state
from copy import deepcopy

store = EventStore()

# --- Define reducers for each event type ---

def cart_created(state: dict, data: dict) -> dict:
    return {"cart_id": data["cart_id"], "items": [], "status": "active"}

def item_added(state: dict, data: dict) -> dict:
    state = deepcopy(state)
    # Check if item already exists, update quantity
    for item in state["items"]:
        if item["sku"] == data["sku"]:
            item["quantity"] += data["quantity"]
            return state
    state["items"].append({
        "sku": data["sku"],
        "name": data["name"],
        "price": data["price"],
        "quantity": data["quantity"],
    })
    return state

def item_removed(state: dict, data: dict) -> dict:
    state = deepcopy(state)
    state["items"] = [i for i in state["items"] if i["sku"] != data["sku"]]
    return state

def checkout_started(state: dict, data: dict) -> dict:
    state = deepcopy(state)
    state["status"] = "checkout"
    return state

def order_placed(state: dict, data: dict) -> dict:
    state = deepcopy(state)
    state["status"] = "ordered"
    state["order_id"] = data["order_id"]
    return state

reducers = {
    "CartCreated": cart_created,
    "ItemAdded": item_added,
    "ItemRemoved": item_removed,
    "CheckoutStarted": checkout_started,
    "OrderPlaced": order_placed,
}

# --- Record events as they happen ---
cart_id = "cart-001"

store.append(cart_id, "CartCreated", {"cart_id": cart_id})
store.append(cart_id, "ItemAdded", {"sku": "SHIRT-M", "name": "T-Shirt (M)", "price": 25.00, "quantity": 2})
store.append(cart_id, "ItemAdded", {"sku": "JEANS-32", "name": "Jeans (32)", "price": 59.99, "quantity": 1})
store.append(cart_id, "ItemRemoved", {"sku": "JEANS-32"})
store.append(cart_id, "ItemAdded", {"sku": "JEANS-34", "name": "Jeans (34)", "price": 59.99, "quantity": 1})
store.append(cart_id, "CheckoutStarted", {})
store.append(cart_id, "OrderPlaced", {"order_id": "ORD-789"})

# --- Rebuild current state by replaying all events ---
current_state = rebuild_state(store, cart_id, reducers)
print(current_state)
# {
#   'cart_id': 'cart-001',
#   'items': [
#     {'sku': 'SHIRT-M', 'name': 'T-Shirt (M)', 'price': 25.0, 'quantity': 2},
#     {'sku': 'JEANS-34', 'name': 'Jeans (34)', 'price': 59.99, 'quantity': 1}
#   ],
#   'status': 'ordered',
#   'order_id': 'ORD-789'
# }

# --- Time travel: rebuild state at any point in history ---
events = store.get_events(cart_id)
for i, event in enumerate(events, 1):
    partial_state = rebuild_state(store, cart_id, reducers)
    print(f"After event {i} ({event.event_type}): {len(partial_state.get('items', []))} items")

blocks.heading: Event Sourcing: Pros and Cons
rows: Complete audit trail -- every change is recorded permanently
Increased complexity -- requires different mental model than CRUD
Time travel -- rebuild state at any point in history
Eventual consistency -- read models may lag behind writes
Event replay -- build new projections from existing events
Storage growth -- events accumulate forever (snapshotting helps)
Debugging -- trace exactly what happened and when
Schema evolution -- changing event schemas requires careful migration
Undo/redo -- reverse events to undo actions
Querying -- cannot easily query current state without projections

blocks.title: Start Simple, Introduce Event Sourcing Selectively
blocks.body: Event sourcing is powerful but complex. Most applications do fine with traditional CRUD. Introduce event sourcing only for domains that genuinely need audit trails, temporal queries, or the ability to replay history -- financial transactions, compliance-sensitive data, collaborative editing, or undo/redo functionality.

blocks.heading: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)
blocks.body: CQRS separates the write side (commands) from the read side (queries). Commands go through validation, business logic, and event processing. Queries hit read-optimized views (projections) that are pre-built from events. This lets you optimize reads and writes independently -- writes can use normalized relational data while reads use denormalized views tuned for specific queries.

blocks.description: CQRS example -- command handler writes events, event handler updates projections, query handler reads projections
blocks.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from event_store import EventStore
from event_dispatcher import dispatcher

store = EventStore()

# --- Read-optimized projections (denormalized views) ---
# In production, these would be database tables or Redis hashes
order_summary_projection: dict[str, dict] = {}  # order_id -> summary
customer_orders_projection: dict[str, list] = {}  # customer_id -> [order summaries]


# === COMMAND SIDE (Writes) ===

@dataclass
class PlaceOrderCommand:
    customer_id: str
    items: list[dict]  # [{"sku": "...", "quantity": N, "price": N}]


def handle_place_order(cmd: PlaceOrderCommand) -> str:
    """Command handler: validate, write events, return order ID."""
    # Validate
    if not cmd.items:
        raise ValueError("Order must have at least one item")

    total = sum(item["price"] * item["quantity"] for item in cmd.items)
    order_id = f"ORD-{len(store.get_all_events()) + 1:04d}"

    # Write event (single source of truth)
    store.append(order_id, "OrderPlaced", {
        "order_id": order_id,
        "customer_id": cmd.customer_id,
        "items": cmd.items,
        "total": total,
    })

    # Emit for async projection update
    dispatcher.emit("OrderPlaced",
        order_id=order_id,
        customer_id=cmd.customer_id,
        items=cmd.items,
        total=total,
    )

    return order_id


# === EVENT HANDLERS (Update Projections) ===

@dispatcher.on("OrderPlaced", lambda **kwargs: update_order_summary(**kwargs))
def update_order_summary(order_id: str, customer_id: str, items: list, total: float, **kw):
    """Update the order summary projection (optimized for order detail page)."""
    order_summary_projection[order_id] = {
        "order_id": order_id,
        "customer_id": customer_id,
        "item_count": sum(i["quantity"] for i in items),
        "total": total,
        "status": "placed",
    }


@dispatcher.on("OrderPlaced", lambda **kwargs: update_customer_orders(**kwargs))
def update_customer_orders(order_id: str, customer_id: str, total: float, **kw):
    """Update the customer orders projection (optimized for 'my orders' page)."""
    if customer_id not in customer_orders_projection:
        customer_orders_projection[customer_id] = []
    customer_orders_projection[customer_id].append({
        "order_id": order_id,
        "total": total,
        "status": "placed",
    })


# === QUERY SIDE (Reads) ===

def get_order_summary(order_id: str) -> dict | None:
    """Query handler: read from projection (fast, denormalized)."""
    return order_summary_projection.get(order_id)


def get_customer_orders(customer_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Query handler: get all orders for a customer."""
    return customer_orders_projection.get(customer_id, [])


# --- Usage ---
order_id = handle_place_order(PlaceOrderCommand(
    customer_id="CUST-42",
    items=[
        {"sku": "WIDGET-A", "quantity": 3, "price": 9.99},
        {"sku": "GADGET-B", "quantity": 1, "price": 49.99},
    ],
))

# Read from optimized projection
print(get_order_summary(order_id))
print(get_customer_orders("CUST-42"))

blocks.body: Most CRUD applications do not need CQRS. If your read and write patterns are similar -- you write a row and read the same row back -- a single model is simpler and perfectly adequate. CQRS adds complexity: you have separate models, eventual consistency between them, and more code to maintain.

Consider CQRS when read and write patterns differ significantly. For example: you write normalized order data (order table, line_items table, customer table) but your dashboard needs a single denormalized view with customer name, order total, item count, and last order date all in one row. Building a read-optimized projection avoids expensive JOINs on every dashboard load.

blocks.heading: Signs You Might Need CQRS
items: Your read queries involve many JOINs or aggregations that are slow
Your write model is heavily normalized but your UI needs denormalized data
Read and write loads differ dramatically (100x more reads than writes)
You need different authorization rules for reading vs writing
You are already using event sourcing and need materialized views

blocks.heading: Real-World Event-Driven Patterns
blocks.body: Production event-driven systems use established patterns to handle failures, ensure reliability, and manage complexity. These patterns have been battle-tested in large-scale distributed systems at companies like Netflix, Uber, and Amazon.

cards.title: Saga / Orchestration
cards.body: Coordinate multi-step workflows across services. If step 3 fails, compensate (undo) steps 1 and 2. Like a booking that reserves flight, hotel, and car -- if the car fails, cancel the rest.

cards.title: Outbox Pattern
cards.body: Write events to a database outbox table in the same transaction as your data change. A separate process reads the outbox and publishes events. Guarantees no events are lost.

cards.title: Dead Letter Queue
cards.body: Messages that fail processing after max retries go to a dead letter queue instead of being discarded. Engineers can inspect, fix, and replay them.

cards.title: Circuit Breaker
cards.body: When a downstream service fails repeatedly, stop sending requests for a cooldown period. Prevents cascading failures across an event chain.

cards.title: Idempotent Consumer
cards.body: Handle duplicate events safely by tracking processed event IDs. At-least-once delivery means you WILL receive duplicates. Your handlers must be safe to run twice.

blocks.description: Idempotent event handler -- using a processed_events table to track and skip duplicates
blocks.code:
import sqlite3
import logging
from functools import wraps

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# In production, use your main database (PostgreSQL, etc.)
db = sqlite3.connect(":memory:", check_same_thread=False)
db.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS processed_events (
        event_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
        event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
        processed_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
        result TEXT
    )
""")


def idempotent(func):
    """Decorator that makes an event handler idempotent.
    
    If the event_id has already been processed, skip it.
    Otherwise, process it and record the event_id.
    """
    @wraps(func)
    def wrapper(event_id: str, event_type: str, **data):
        # Check if already processed
        row = db.execute(
            "SELECT 1 FROM processed_events WHERE event_id = ?",
            (event_id,),
        ).fetchone()

        if row:
            logger.info(f"Skipping duplicate event: {event_id} ({event_type})")
            return None

        # Process the event
        try:
            result = func(event_id=event_id, event_type=event_type, **data)

            # Record as processed
            db.execute(
                "INSERT INTO processed_events (event_id, event_type, result) VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
                (event_id, event_type, str(result)),
            )
            db.commit()

            return result
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error(f"Failed to process event {event_id}: {e}")
            raise  # Do NOT mark as processed -- it should be retried

    return wrapper


@idempotent
def handle_payment_received(event_id: str, event_type: str, order_id: str, amount: float, **kwargs):
    """Process a payment -- safe to call multiple times with the same event_id."""
    logger.info(f"Processing payment for order {order_id}: ${amount}")
    # Update order status, send receipt, etc.
    return {"order_id": order_id, "status": "paid"}


# First call: processes the event
handle_payment_received(
    event_id="evt_001", event_type="payment.received",
    order_id="ORD-42", amount=99.99,
)
# Output: Processing payment for order ORD-42: $99.99

# Second call with same event_id: skipped
handle_payment_received(
    event_id="evt_001", event_type="payment.received",
    order_id="ORD-42", amount=99.99,
)
# Output: Skipping duplicate event: evt_001 (payment.received)

blocks.heading: Debugging Event-Driven Systems
blocks.body: Event-driven systems are harder to debug because the flow is not linear. In request/response, you can follow the call stack from endpoint to service to database. In event-driven systems, the producer does not know who handles the event, events may be processed out of order, and failures can happen silently in background workers. You need observability.

blocks.heading: Essential Debugging Tools
items: **Structured logging with correlation IDs** -- Attach a unique ID to every event in a chain. When user action X triggers events A, B, and C, all three log entries share the same correlation ID so you can trace the full flow.
**Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry)** -- Traces follow events across service boundaries. Visualize the entire event chain as a single trace with timing for each step.
**Event replay** -- Store events and replay them against fixed handlers to reproduce bugs. Event sourcing makes this natural.
**Dead letter queue monitoring** -- Set up alerts when events land in the DLQ. These are events that failed all retry attempts and need manual investigation.
**Event schema validation** -- Validate event payloads against a schema before processing. Catch malformed events early instead of getting cryptic errors downstream.
**Event catalog / registry** -- Document every event type, its schema, its producers, and its consumers. Without this, nobody knows what events exist or who listens to them.

blocks.description: Correlation ID middleware -- attach a unique ID to every event for end-to-end tracing
blocks.code:
import uuid
import logging
from contextvars import ContextVar
from flask import Flask, request, g
from functools import wraps

# Context variable for correlation ID (thread-safe)
correlation_id_var: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("correlation_id", default="")


class CorrelationFilter(logging.Filter):
    """Inject correlation_id into every log record."""
    def filter(self, record):
        record.correlation_id = correlation_id_var.get("no-correlation-id")
        return True


# Configure logging with correlation ID
handler = logging.StreamHandler()
handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter(
    "%(asctime)s [%(correlation_id)s] %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s"
))
handler.addFilter(CorrelationFilter())

logger = logging.getLogger("app")
logger.addHandler(handler)
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)


# --- Flask middleware ---
app = Flask(__name__)

@app.before_request
def set_correlation_id():
    """Extract correlation ID from header or generate a new one."""
    cid = request.headers.get("X-Correlation-ID", str(uuid.uuid4()))
    correlation_id_var.set(cid)
    g.correlation_id = cid


@app.after_request
def add_correlation_header(response):
    """Include correlation ID in the response for client-side tracing."""
    response.headers["X-Correlation-ID"] = g.get("correlation_id", "")
    return response


# --- Use in event handlers ---
def with_correlation(func):
    """Decorator to pass correlation ID through event chains."""
    @wraps(func)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        # If a correlation ID is passed in the event data, use it
        cid = kwargs.pop("correlation_id", None) or str(uuid.uuid4())
        correlation_id_var.set(cid)
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
    return wrapper


@with_correlation
def handle_order_placed(order_id: str, **kwargs):
    logger.info(f"Processing order: {order_id}")
    # When emitting downstream events, include the correlation ID:
    # dispatcher.emit("order.confirmed", order_id=order_id,
    #                 correlation_id=correlation_id_var.get())


# Example log output with correlation IDs:
# 2025-01-15 10:30:01 [a1b2c3d4-...] INFO app: Processing order: ORD-42
# 2025-01-15 10:30:01 [a1b2c3d4-...] INFO app: Sending confirmation email
# 2025-01-15 10:30:02 [a1b2c3d4-...] INFO app: Updating inventory
# All three lines share the same correlation ID for easy tracing.

blocks.title: Invest in Observability Early
blocks.body: The biggest challenge with event-driven systems is understanding what happened and in what order. When a customer reports 'my order is stuck', you need to trace the event chain from order placement through payment processing to fulfillment. Without correlation IDs, structured logging, and distributed tracing, this is nearly impossible. Set up observability before you need it, not after a production incident.

blocks.heading: Choosing the Right Pattern
blocks.body: There is no single best event pattern. Each solves a different problem. Use this decision matrix to pick the right approach for your specific scenario.

blocks.heading: Event Pattern Decision Matrix
rows: Both client and server need to send messages at any time. Bidirectional, persistent connection.
Server pushes updates, client only listens. Simpler than WebSocket, auto-reconnects.
CPU-intensive work must not block the web request. Queue with retries ensures completion.
External service POSTs events to your endpoint. Verify signature and process.
Need complete history of every change, time travel, and replay capability.
Decouple React components that do not share a parent. Use Redux/Zustand for complex state.
Decouple services, buffer messages, guarantee delivery even if consumer is temporarily down.
High throughput, ordered, durable log. Process millions of events per second.
